title: Emergence of Macroscopic Quantum Order via Translational Zero Modes
abstract: Macroscopic quantum coherence in solids, such as in superfluids, superconductors, and condensates, is generally limited to low temperatures because order forms within a fixed excitation spectrum whose competing states become thermally populated as temperature rises. Here, we show that strong coupling between electronic excitations and a deformable lattice enables a different route. Above a critical density, this coupling nucleates self generated confining potentials that trap the very excitations generating them. Unlike rigid external traps, these potentials can translate through the host lattice without changing their internal structure, defining a translational zero mode. Coupling to this zero mode provides a shared dynamical coordinate that lowers and isolates a single collective many body configuration, opening a density dependent gap that suppresses thermal occupation of competing states and supports off diagonal long range order at elevated temperatures. As a concrete realization, we identify high-temperature superfluorescence in lead halide perovskites as the radiative instability of this zero mode dressed ordered excitonic state. More broadly, this establishes a general route to macroscopic quantum order: not cooling within a fixed spectrum, nor pairing instabilities, but a self generated mobile confining structure whose translational zero mode reconstructs the many-body spectrum to protect coherence.
summary: The paper proposes that macroscopic quantum order can be sustained at high temperatures not by cooling, but by the self-generation of a mobile confining potential (soliton) coupled to the lattice. This coupling creates a 'translational zero mode' that opens a protective energy gap, stabilizing the collective quantum state and enhancing the critical temperature beyond conventional predictions.
main_problem: To find a mechanism for achieving macroscopic quantum order at elevated temperatures that bypasses the limitations of fixed excitation spectra or conventional pairing instabilities.
main_result: The coupling of electronic excitations to a deformable lattice via a translational zero mode generates a self-confining potential, opening a density-dependent gap that stabilizes coherence at higher temperatures.
method: The analysis uses effective Hamiltonian descriptions, mean-field theory, and variational principles to model the collective energy lowering driven by the zero mode.
model_or_system: The system involves excitons strongly coupled to a deformable lattice, leading to a density-induced lattice instability that creates a mobile, self-generated solitonic potential. This potential gives rise to a translational zero mode.
key_observables: Density-dependent gap ($\Delta_{gap}$), critical temperature ($T_c$), and the occupation of the single collective many-body orbital ($\lambda_0 = O(N)$).
important_parameters: Critical density, coupling constants ($g_1, g_2$), and the zero-mode stiffness ($\kappa$).
main_assumptions: The zero mode acts as a shared collective coordinate, and the system can be effectively described by a two-level subspace in the macroscopic limit.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the transition from uniform to solitonic phases, the modification of the Bose phase-space factor due to the gap, and the enhancement of $T_c$ beyond standard scaling laws.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from identifying the general problem to modeling the lattice instability, deriving the zero-mode coupling, calculating the resulting energy lowering, and finally demonstrating the stabilization of coherence via the gap and ODLRO.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, general theoretical framework for achieving high-temperature quantum coherence by engineering structural degrees of freedom (the zero mode) to modify the many-body spectrum, which is highly relevant to AMO physics and quantum optics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 From fixed-spectrum condensation to zero-mode-stabilized collective order. (a) In conventional condensation, particles occupy the lowest level of a fixed excitation spectrum only after thermal occupation of higher-energy states is reduced below a critical temperature. (b) In a solitonic confining potential, fluctuations of the translational zero mode shift the potential relative to the laboratory frame. In the soliton frame, this motion coherently mixes the defect-bound excitonic states. (c) In the many-body regime, all confined excitons couple to the same shared zero-mode coordinate. This collective structural channel drives a single many-body state |ΨN⟩to lower energy, with...

Fig. 2 Density-induced lattice instability and formation of a self-generated solitonic potential. (a) Effective Landau free energy Feff as a function of the lattice deformation coordinate φ for increasing excitation density. At low density, the uniform configuration φ = 0 is the stable minimum. As the density increases, the quadratic exciton–lattice coupling g2 renormalizes the effec- tive quartic coefficient ueff, causing the free-energy landscape to develop finite-deformation minima. At sufficiently high density, a finite-φ configuration becomes energetically favored, indicating a ther- modynamic instability of the uniform lattice. (b) Real-space representation of the transition. In the...

Fig. 3 Impact of the solitonic transition on excitonic spatial coherence and potential landscapes. (a) In the homogeneous regime, the uniform crystal lattice defines an unconfined con- tinuous spectrum, supporting extended excitonic eigenstates approximated here as plane waves. (b) Extended excitons interact with a gapless continuum of traveling thermal phonons. These low-energy fluctuations generate random lattice distortions that modulate the potential landscape, dephase the excitonic wavefunctions, and distribute the population over many spatially incoherent configurations.
